content:
Before ReLU became dominant, hidden layers in neural networks typically used **sigmoid** or **tanh**. Both squash inputs into bounded ranges, but their derivatives shrink toward zero in saturated regions—multiplying many such derivatives through deep stacks caused **vanishing gradients** and painfully slow training.

**Rectified linear** ideas existed earlier, but **ReLU** gained wide adoption after **Nair and Hinton** (2010) showed rectified linear units improved training of restricted Boltzmann machines. **Krizhevsky, Sutskever, and Hinton**'s **AlexNet** (2012) used ReLU in a deep convolutional network and demonstrated dramatically faster training on GPUs compared to tanh—cementing ReLU as the default hidden activation in computer vision.

The **dying ReLU** phenomenon was recognized as networks grew deeper: neurons that fell into the always-off region stopped learning. **Leaky ReLU** (**Maas et al.**, 2013) introduced a small fixed negative slope. **PReLU** (**He et al.**, 2015, "Delving Deep into Rectifiers") made the negative slope a learnable parameter per channel, paired with **He initialization** designed for ReLU-family activations.

In modern Gen AI, **transformers** often prefer **GELU** in feed-forward blocks, but ReLU and Leaky ReLU remain standard in CNNs, efficient vision backbones, and many MLP components. The ReLU family addressed the immediate predecessor problem—saturated sigmoid/tanh hidden units—while spawning variants that trade simplicity for robustness against dead neurons.

timeline.label: Saturated activations era (pre-2010)
timeline.description: Sigmoid and tanh dominated hidden layers; deep training suffered from vanishing gradients in saturated regions.

timeline.label: ReLU popularized (Nair & Hinton, 2010)
timeline.description: Rectified linear units shown to train faster than sigmoid in restricted Boltzmann machines.

timeline.label: AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)
timeline.description: Deep CNN with ReLU hidden activations wins ImageNet; ReLU becomes the vision default.

timeline.label: Leaky ReLU (Maas et al., 2013)
timeline.description: Fixed small negative slope proposed to reduce dying ReLU units.

timeline.label: PReLU & He init (He et al., 2015)
timeline.description: Learnable per-channel negative slopes and initialization matched to ReLU-family activations.
